elements.sent-2411-001: The opening or closing mechanism of a voltage-gated ion channel is triggered by the potential difference crossing the cell membrane in the nervous system.
elements.sent-2411-002: Based on this picture, we model the ion channel as a nanoscale two-terminal ionic tunneling junction.
elements.sent-2411-003: External time-varying voltage exerting on the two-terminal ionic tunneling junction mimics the stimulation of neurons from the outside.
elements.sent-2411-004: By deriving the quantum Langevin equation from quantum mechanics, the ion channel current is obtained by the quantum tunneling of ions controlled by the time-varying voltage.
elements.sent-2411-005: The time-varying voltage induces an effective magnetic flux which causes quantum coherence in ion tunnelings and leads to sideband effects in the ion channel current dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-006: The sideband effects in the ionic current dynamics manifest a multi-crossing hysteresis in the I-V curve, which is the memory dynamics responding to the variation of the external voltage.
elements.sent-2411-007: Such memory dynamics is defined as the active quantum memory with respect to the time-varying stimuli.
elements.sent-2411-008: We can quantitatively describe how active quantum memory is generated and changed.
elements.sent-2411-009: We find that the number of the non-zero cross points in the I-V curve hysteresis and the oscillation of the differential conductance are the characteristics for quantitatively describing the active quantum memory.
elements.sent-2411-010: We also explore the temperature dependence of the active quantum memory in such a system.
elements.sent-2411-011: The discovery of this active quantum memory characteristics provides a new understanding about the underlying mechanism of ion channel dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-012: It is widely accepted that nerve status can be demonstrated by the electrical signals carried via potential difference between the inside and outside of the cell membrane.
elements.sent-2411-013: The corresponding ion channel responses dominate the overall changes.
elements.sent-2411-014: This picture makes the nervous system look like a circuit system.
elements.sent-2411-015: There are plenty of ion channels on the neuron membrane, and each voltage-gated ion channel is usually designed as a simple circuit in electrophysiology experiments.
elements.sent-2411-016: From the electrophysiological perspective, researchers have focused on establishing a practically useful circuit theory to describe the behavior of ion channels.
elements.sent-2411-017: The prototypical example is the HH model, proposed by Hodgkin and Huxley \cite{HH utilizing the nonlinear RC circuit, and its extensions which have successfully depicted the conductance of various ion channel clusters observed in experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-018: On the other hand, looking into the protein structure of a single ion channel which can be analyzed through the X-ray crystallography, cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) or high-resolution NMR techniques \cite{Do,AH,Mac,Ca,MBYWA, SMSS2018, PZT2021, MZG2021, GH2021 , the ions are transported through a single ion channel with an tiny pore.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The aperture of a single ion channel is ranged from $1\sim15 $\AA ~\cite{KSTMM,MSAHMH,SNWWS,AH,KRST , while some special large conductance ion channels could reach to pore size about $40$ \AA $=4$ nm ~\cite{CMDM .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: The nano- and subnano-scale (atomic-scale) ion channels restrict ions flow into the channels individually with the selectivity \cite{DMCPK,MZM,ZMCKM .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Under such nano- and subnano-scale and selective ability, the quantumness may play an important role in the neural signal transmission \cite{RL, SSB, SNS, GCE, GSS .}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, among the works mentioned above, a general quantum biological theory has not yet been developed to explain the mechanism of neural signal transmission through ion channels.
elements.sent-2411-023: In the literature, it has been investigated the dynamics of ion transmission utilizing the classical transport theory \cite{FBL,CJW, Z .}
elements.sent-2411-024: We believe that the quantumness should be taken into account from the fundamental level of quantum theory for the nano-scale ion channels and the sub-nanoscale (atomic) ion dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-025: A single ion channel can be analogous to a quantum molecular or atomic junction between the intracellular and extracellular ionic solutions.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this work, we propose a resonant level tunneling junction to depict such ionic junction for ion channel dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-027: The resonant level tunneling system in nanoelectronics is made with a double barrier coupled with two electrode leads and forced by external voltages.
elements.sent-2411-028: The central region between the double barriers are described as an artificial atom with quantized energy levels.
elements.sent-2411-029: Consequently, this physical process is analogous to the ions transmitting through a single ion channel (Fig.~\ref{fig1:ion channel ).}
elements.sent-2411-030: The two electrode leads in the resonant level tunneling system correspond to the intracellular and extracellular ionic solutions in ion channels.
elements.sent-2411-031: In the last decade, we have developed a very general non-equilibrium time-dependent quantum transport theory \cite{TuZhang2008,JTZY, LZ2012,YLZ,YZ2017,YLZ2023 for nanoelectronics and quantum materials.}
elements.sent-2411-032: This allows us to analyze the ion transport phenomenon in the nervous system microscopically in terms of non-equilibrium quantum transport dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-033: In this non-equilibrium quantum transport dynamical theory, the ion tunnelings through the ion channel and the associated memory effects can be analyzed quantum mechanically.
elements.sent-2411-034: This could provide a new research avenue to investigate the quantum memory dynamics of ion channels in the nervous system.
elements.sent-2411-035: If the evolution of a system is determined by the time-convolution over all its past historical evolutions, we say that the evolution of the system is a memory process.
elements.sent-2411-036: The concept of memory originally came from the learning and cognitive process of human brain.
elements.sent-2411-037: A specific memory concept related to learning and cognition is the so-called "working memory" which was first proposed by Miller, Galanter, and Pribram in 1960 \cite{MGP , and has been described in many models \cite{AS,BH1974,Bad,Cow1988,Cow1999 .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Among those investigations, there are two influential models in different directions have been proposed, the multi-component memory model and the active long-term memory model.
elements.sent-2411-039: The multi-component memory model says that the working memory is dominated by the central executive and interacts with three parts: the episodic buffer, the phonological loop, and the visuospatial sketchpad \cite{Bad .}
elements.sent-2411-040: The active long-term memory model more directly incorporates working memory into long-term memory (LTM) \cite{Cow1988,EK,Cow1999,Cowan .}
elements.sent-2411-041: LTM contains the knowledge acquired in the past and significant events in our lives.
elements.sent-2411-042: These rich LTMs come from short-term memories (STMs) and are formed by repeatedly learning or suffering events that have a great impact.
elements.sent-2411-043: For example, when memorizing English vocabulary, one relies on repeated reading, writing, or using prefixes or suffixes to make our memory more profound.
elements.sent-2411-044: The existence of STM allows one to possess some data for a short period of time and conduct in-depth thinking by tracing back the LTM in the past, and then forming a new LTM.
elements.sent-2411-045: Although the architectures are different, the concepts of working memory are the same, basically for repeatedly generating memories through external influences with a phenomenological and qualitative approach.
elements.sent-2411-046: It still lacks a quantitative description of dynamical memory mechanisms based on fundamental physics principles at the molecular and atomic levels.
elements.sent-2411-047: On the other hand, with the rapid development of quantum science and technology, in particular, quantum computing and quantum information in the last two decades, there are extensive investigations of open quantum systems with the dissipative memory (also called the non-Markovian memory) induced by the system-environment backactions.
elements.sent-2411-048: From the open quantum system point of view, the relevant system of interest interacts with surrounding environments, causing the dissipative and decoherent quantum dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-049: In particular, for many surrounding environments, especially for the structural or engineering environments, the decoherence is mainly dominated by various non-Markovian memory dynamics of quantum processes \cite{ZLXTN,ALTZ,VA,WMZ2019 , where the memory incorporates all the backactions from environments.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Each environment can be characterized uniquely by a spectral density function of the corresponding physical systems \cite{Leggett1987 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: It describes in details the interactions between the system and its environment and the environmental quantum spectral structure \cite{WMZ2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-052: To explore neural signal transmissions in the nervous system, ion channels embedded in the cell membrane are treated as the systems of interest, and the intracellular and extracellular ionic solution near the ion channels are considered as the surrounding environments.
elements.sent-2411-053: To mimics the stimulation of neurons from the outside, we exerts external time-varying voltages on the intracellular and extracellular ionic solution (the two side of the ionic junction) as the outside and inside potentials of the membrane.
elements.sent-2411-054: The time-varying voltages can change significantly the original spectral density structure of the intracellular and extracellular ionic solutions.
elements.sent-2411-055: In particular, the external time-varying voltage induces effective magnetic flux phase for ion tunnelings passing through the ion channel.
elements.sent-2411-056: These effective magnetic flux-associated phases generate the quantum coherence in the ion tunneling dynamics and lead to an additional memory effect.
elements.sent-2411-057: We call this memory effect as the active quantum memory.
elements.sent-2411-058: The phenomenon of active quantum memory can be observed in the ion transmission processes.
elements.sent-2411-059: It results in a ringing transport current in the ion channel, referred to as the sideband effect \cite{WJM,JWM,JTZY .}
elements.sent-2411-060: The sideband effect manifested in the transient ionic current is the strong evidence of the circuit carrying with quantum memory and nonlinearity, demonstrated by the emergence of the multi-crossing hysteresis in the I-V curve.
elements.sent-2411-061: The emergence of the multi-crossing I-V curve hysteresis in the ion transport gives rise to a quantitative description of the active quantum memory effect in the nervous system, as it is responding to the external potential changes.
elements.sent-2411-062: Furthermore, in the ionic tunneling junction, the quantized energy level sensitively makes the current transmittance rate vary following the changes of external potential.
elements.sent-2411-063: It is the current increasing and decreasing repeatedly that forms the sideband effect.
elements.sent-2411-064: Correspondingly, there occurs negative differential conductance which has been observed in the resonant tunneling system \cite{TE,CET,SGTPP,Lu,CMC,HGF,TMH,PFK .}
elements.sent-2411-065: The time-dependent current was indeed investigated extensively in nanoelectronics \cite{JTZY,WJM, JWM, KT, DDL,YZ2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: Here, in terms of open quantum system approach, we utilize the equation of motion approach to study the ion channel current and the corresponding differential conductance \cite{YLZ,YZ2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: Our goal is to quantitatively characterize the amount of active quantum memory in the ion channel current.
elements.sent-2411-068: Furthermore, the temperature dependency of the active quantum memory is also explored in this work.
elements.sent-2411-069: We believe that this formulation of active quantum memory in ion channel dynamics is homologous with the concept of working memory induced from the external stimuli in the nervous system.
elements.sent-2411-070: Also, for the practical applications, developing such a theory for quantum memory circuits should be useful in the development of neuromorphic computing technology in the future.
elements.sent-2411-071: The neuromorphic computing is a brain-inspired computation framework different from the von Neumann architecture.
elements.sent-2411-072: It has the ability of speeding up the conventional computation, reducing the energy consumption, and making the computer flexible for learning.
elements.sent-2411-073: In the hardware aspect, memristor has been considered as a key component for neuromorphic computation.
elements.sent-2411-074: The concept of memristor was proposed by Leon O.
elements.sent-2411-075: Chua in 1971, representing a time-dependent resistor changed with memory \cite{Chua, IC,SSSW .}
elements.sent-2411-076: The implementation of a memristor is to use a mutator with a nonlinear passive element (a resistor, a capacitor or a inductor) to create a nonlinear I-V relation $V(t) = I(t)M(Q(t))$ with $M(Q(t))=d\Phi(t)/dQ(t) = V(t)/I(t)$, where the charge $Q(t) = \int_{-\infty ^t I(\tau)d\tau$ and the magnetic flux $\Phi(t) = \int_{-\infty ^t V(\tau)d\tau$, and the forward and backward currents have different resistances.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: This property saves the status of the circuit and forms the memory.
elements.sent-2411-078: Since the difference between current and voltage is only a nonlinear multiplier, the I-V characteristics always pass the zero point ($V=0,I=0$).
elements.sent-2411-079: In Chua's idea, memristor is defined as the fourth basic passive element by defining an equation connecting charge and magnetic flux.
elements.sent-2411-080: However, this concept remains a subject of controversy and ongoing debate within the scientific community.
elements.sent-2411-081: It still lacks the fundamental physical principle for the necessarily of introducing the fourth basic passive element in circuits.
elements.sent-2411-082: Although many investigations suggest that the memristor functions as a resistor with memory, only a limited number of studies have undertaken an in-depth investigation to unravel the fundamental nature of this memory phenomenon and delve into the physical principles \cite{Abra,TBCM .}
elements.sent-2411-083: Hence, one of our objectives is to construct a quantum memory circuit for depicting the ion channel dynamics and investigating quantitatively the memory effect, in particular for searching the source of a memory circuit and understanding the diverse memory phenomena in greater depth.
elements.sent-2411-084: The rest of the paper is organized as follows, In Sec.~II, springing from the structure of ion channels, we introduce the two-terminal ionic quantum tunneling junction.
elements.sent-2411-085: By applying time-varying voltages on the two sides of the ion channel, the corresponding transient ion channel current is formulated in details.
elements.sent-2411-086: The ion tunnelings through the junction carry different quantum phases associated with effective magnetic fluxes which are induced by the external time-varying voltages.
elements.sent-2411-087: In Sec.~III, we present analytic and numerical results under the wide-band limit of the spectral density for intracellular and extracellular ionic solution.
elements.sent-2411-088: The corresponding ion channel current displays sideband effect which results in the multi-crossing hysteresis in the I-V curve, as a quantum coherence effect representing the active quantum memory dynamics via the time-varying voltages.
elements.sent-2411-089: A critical parameter, the effective magnetic flux strength, is demonstrated for quantitatively counting the degree of the active quantum memory.
elements.sent-2411-090: Also, the amount of memory is characterized by the number of the non-zero-crossing points in the I-V curve hysteresis.
elements.sent-2411-091: The steady-state differential conductance is also analyzed for the different degree of active quantum memory.
elements.sent-2411-092: In addition, we also study the temperature dependence of the memory effect.
elements.sent-2411-093: From the structure of the I-V characteristics we obtained, we find that the concept of memristor does not exist in our quantum memory circuit.
elements.sent-2411-094: Then, we implement this theory to a realistic system.
elements.sent-2411-095: Finally, discussions and prespectives are made in Sec.~IV.

elements.sent-2411-272: In this work, we construct a quantum memory circuit for the ion channel dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-273: We model a single ion channel as an ionic tunneling junction and study the ionic quantum transport through the ion channel.
elements.sent-2411-274: Using the quantum Langevin equation approach of open quantum system and the non-equilibrium Green's function method, we compute the ion quantum transport current passing through a single ion channel.
elements.sent-2411-275: The ion dynamics in the ion channel is determined by the time-convolution equation of motion derived for open quantum systems, and thereby provides a quantum memory description through the ion channel current.
elements.sent-2411-276: We find that the external time-varying voltage exerting on the intracellular and extracellular ionic solution near the ion channels induce an effective magnetic flux phase to the ion tunnelings and cause the quantum coherent ion transmission.
elements.sent-2411-277: We show that the effective flux phases induce quantum coherence in ion tunnelings and dominates the memory dynamics in the ion transport, in responding to the external time-varying voltage.
elements.sent-2411-278: We define this memory dynamics as the active quantum memory.
elements.sent-2411-279: We show that the active quantum memory is associated with the sideband oscillations in the ion channel current, which provide a way how to observe the active quantum memory in experiments.
elements.sent-2411-280: We also demonstrate that the sideband oscillation structure is a significant manifestation of the quantum coherence through the effective magnetic fluxes induced by the external time-varying voltages, as the main physical origin of the active memory dynamics in the ion channel dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-281: The active quantum memory also manifests the multipole non-zero crossing points in the I-V curve hysteresis and the oscillating differential conductance.
elements.sent-2411-282: This may provide a underlying quantum description about the connection of the active quantum memory in the ion channel dynamics with the external stimuli of the neuron membrane in the nervous system.
elements.sent-2411-283: Throughout this study, we aim to connect a quantum memory circuit to the neurotransmission in ion channels.
